key_insight: A machine-learning potential reveals that Mg transmutation products and intrinsic defects in 3C-SiC suppress thermal conductivity in strongly configuration-dependent ways, with vacancy-containing defects acting as the most severe phonon scatterers.
engineering_relevance: The results provide a quantitative atomistic basis for predicting and managing heat transport degradation in SiC nuclear ceramics under irradiation and transmutation.
method: Trained MLIP4SiC-Mg on a large DFT dataset for pristine SiC, intrinsic defects, Mg defects, and Mg-defect complexes.
Validated the model against DFT energies, forces, equation of state, phonon dispersions, and lattice thermal conductivities.
Used Green-Kubo MD with force-error correction and a resistance-based framework to compute thermal conductivity in large defective supercells.
evidence_signal: The corrected pristine 3C-SiC thermal conductivity is 421 W/(mK) at 300 K, in good agreement with experiment, supporting near-DFT accuracy across the evaluated properties.
watch_item: Defect resistivity is not strictly linear with concentration, so dilute-defect extrapolations and mixed-defect clustering effects require careful treatment.
paper_title: Thermal Transport in SiC with Intrinsic Defects and Mg Transmutation Products
